sections.heading: WHO Classification
sections.content: Breast cancer is a malignant neoplasm arising from the epithelial cells of the breast. According to the WHO Classification of Tumors, 5th Edition (2019), breast tumors are classified into in situ carcinomas and invasive carcinomas. The breast is an organ with a well-defined embryological origin from the mammary ridge, and malignancy can develop from any component of the ductal or lobular structures.

sections.heading: TNM Staging
sections.content: The T category of the primary tumor is defined primarily by the size of the invasive component of the cancer, based on the maximum size of a tumor focus as an estimate of disease volume [ST-1]. T1 tumors measure 20 mm or less in greatest dimension (T1mi ≤1 mm, T1a >1–5 mm, T1b >5–10 mm, T1c >10–20 mm); T2 tumors measure >20 mm but ≤50 mm; T3 tumors measure >50 mm; and T4 tumors extend to the chest wall or skin, including T4d inflammatory carcinoma. Lobular carcinoma in situ (LCIS) is a benign entity and is removed from TNM staging in the AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition [ST-1].

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.content: The diagnosis is confirmed by histopathologic examination with determination of hormone receptor status (ER and PR) and HER2 status, following standardized reporting protocols endorsed by the College of American Pathologists [BINV-1]. A clinical diagnosis of inflammatory breast cancer (IBC) requires erythema and dermal edema (peau d'orange) of a third or more of the breast skin, with pathologic confirmation of invasive cancer [IBC-1].
